coredata.dc:title: In vivo toxicity assessment of four types of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using mRNA sequencing
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Shun
Zhang, Enming
Wang, Yan
Zhao, Yunyang
Yang, Zezhong
Zheng, Bingxin
Mu, Xiaoyuan
Deng, Xuangen
Shen, Hai
Rong, Haibo
Pei, Desheng
coredata.dc:description: GQDs show great potential in drug carriers, bioimaging, biosensors, theranostics, and are recently reported as promising therapeutic agents to treat amyloid-related diseases such as Parkinson’s disease and inflammations such as colitis. However, current toxicity data about GQDs based on in vivo toxicity assessments remain scarce. In the study, we examined the mRNA expression changes of zebrafish embryos exposed to four types of GQDs, including raw graphene quantum dots (R-GQDs), graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), carboxyl GQDs (C-GQDs), and aminated GQDs (A-GQDs). Firstly, we treated embryos with the four GQDs at three concentrations (50, 100, and 200 μg/mL), and found that only A-GQDs caused embryonic developmental arrest at 100 and 200 μg/mL with significantly decreased survival rates and heartbeat rates, as well as the elevated malformation rates. Next, we analyzed the mRNA sequencing data acquired from zebrafish embryos exposed to the four GQDs for 7 days at 100 μg/mL, and found that all GQDs can act on potassium (K+) and calcium (Ca2+) channels, and spliceosomes with varying degrees of regulatory effects. Compared to other GQDs, A-GQDs can strongly perturb the anticoagulant protein C (PC) pathway via activating most genes associated with complement and coagulation system, cell adhesion molecules (CAMs), and MAPK. In conclusion, this study provided substantial transcriptomic data underlying the common signaling pathways induced by various types of GQDs and pointed out the specific toxicity of A-GQDs on hemostatic system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Toxicity
Zebrafish
Transcriptomic responses

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of calcium and ATP levels in zebrafish embryos
The disruptive effect of A-GQDs on hemostatic system
qRT-PCR assays and WB for A-GQDs toxicity on hemostasis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface Characterization of various GQDs. (A) TEM image of R-GQDs (a1), GOQDs (a2), C-GQDs (a3), and A-GQDs (a4). Insert shows the size distribution and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) of the corresponding GQDs, The scale bar in HRTEM is 2 nm. (B) The wide-scan XPS spectra for GQDs and three elemental peaks (N1s, C1s, and O1s) are marked in the spectra profiles. (C) C1s and O1s spectra profiles of GQDs, including R-GQDs, GOQDs, C-GQDs, and A-GQDs. (D) zeta potential (eV) of four GQDs in three mediums, including ultrapure water (ddH2O), 1 × PBS, and 1 × DMEM.

Developmental toxicity assessment of zebrafish embryos exposed to different GQDs treatment. (A-D) Survival rates were calculated from 2 dpf to 7 dpf and each GQDs treatment was performed in triplicates (n = 50 embryos per replicate), A: R-GQDs, B: GOQDs, C: C-GQDs, D: A-GQDs. Heartbeat rates (beats/min) were counted at 3 dpf (E) and 4 dpf (F) using an automatic counter on three independent replicates (n = 6–8 embryos per replicate) for each treatment. All experimental data were expressed with Mean ± SD and the level of p < 0.05 (*) was considered to be significant.

RNA-seq data analysis. (A) PCA of mRNA expression profiling among various treatment as indicated by different symbols. (B) Statistical results of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between control and different GQDs treatment based on a FDR value of < 0.05 and a fold change (FC) of > 1.5, red and black column represents the up- and down-regulated number of mRNAs. (C) Venn analysis of the DEGs from various GQDs treatment.

Gene ontology (GO) analysis for DEGs induced by various GQDs treatment. (A) Top ten GO terms generalized from each GQDs treatment, both were screened out with a FDR value of < 0.05. (B) Venn analysis based on the significantly enriched GO terms from respective GQDs treatment, and Venn analysis of the number of the overlapping DEGs induced in the GO term- ‘ion membrane transport’ (C) and ‘ion transport’ (D), respectively. (E) A summary of the overlapping genes screened out from (C) and (D) depicted by heatmap. (F) Detection of calcium levels in zebrafish embryos exposed to various GQDs at 100 μg/mL at 6 dpf. (G) ATP production of zebrafish embryonic cells treated by different GQDs at 100 μg/mL at 6 dpf. (H) Western blotting analysis of CAMIIα expression levels of the whole embryonic extracts tissues in different GQDs at 100 μg/mL at 6 dpf. (I) The relative expression of CAMIIα was normalized against β-tublin according to the integrated intensity of the WB bands which were analyzed with the Image J software, and three independent replicates were performed for each treatment.

KEGG pathway analysis for DEGs induced by various GQDs treatment. (A) The significantly enriched KEGG pathways generalized from each GQDs treatment, both were screened out with a FDR value of < 0.05. (B) Heatmap depiction of the overlapping DEGs among four GQDs treatment. (C) The common signaling pathways shared by the four GQDs treatments, which were deduced from the online String database and extracted from Cytoscape 3.8.0, the insert table exhibits the interconnected nodes for different genes.

Toxicity pathways induced in zebrafish embryos exposed to A-GQDs. (A) A portray of gene interaction network induced by A-GQDs at 100 μg/mL, two gene clusters were deduced from the combined analysis of online String tool and Cytoscape 3.8 software. (B) Heatmap shows the gene cluster ascribed to anticoagulant protein C (PC) pathway triggered by A-GQDs at 100 μg/mL, and the significant change between control and treatment is presented as follows: *FDR < 0.05, ** FDR < 0.01, *** FDR < 0.001. (C) In vivo imaging of the trunk vasculature of 4 dpf embryos (fli: EGFP) exposed to different GQDs at 100 μg/mL. (D) The comparison of the relative fluorescent intensity (RFI) between control and different GQDs at 100 μg/mL.

qPCR verification and WB analysis. (A) 9 marker genes related to PC pathway and (pro-) apoptotic signals, including proca, c3a, f5, plg, thbd, vcam1, tgfb2, fli1, fas, and cox4. Each treatment was carried out with four biological replicates. (B-C) Western blotting for CASP8 expression levels of zebrafish embryos exposed to different GQDs at 100 μg/mL. The comparison between control and different GQDs treatment was statistically analyzed with Independent-sample t-test, * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.

head.ce:title: In vivo toxicity assessment of four types of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using mRNA sequencing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Scientific Research Center, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325035, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: GQDs show great potential in drug carriers, bioimaging, biosensors, theranostics, and are recently reported as promising therapeutic agents to treat amyloid-related diseases such as Parkinson’s disease and inflammations such as colitis. However, current toxicity data about GQDs based on in vivo toxicity assessments remain scarce. In the study, we examined the mRNA expression changes of zebrafish embryos exposed to four types of GQDs, including raw graphene quantum dots (R-GQDs), graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), carboxyl GQDs (C-GQDs), and aminated GQDs (A-GQDs). Firstly, we treated embryos with the four GQDs at three concentrations (50, 100, and 200 μg/mL), and found that only A-GQDs caused embryonic developmental arrest at 100 and 200 μg/mL with significantly decreased survival rates and heartbeat rates, as well as the elevated malformation rates. Next, we analyzed the mRNA sequencing data acquired from zebrafish embryos exposed to the four GQDs for 7 days at 100 μg/mL, and found that all GQDs can act on potassium (K+) and calcium (Ca2+) channels, and spliceosomes with varying degrees of regulatory effects. Compared to other GQDs, A-GQDs can strongly perturb the anticoagulant protein C (PC) pathway via activating most genes associated with complement and coagulation system, cell adhesion molecules (CAMs), and MAPK. In conclusion, this study provided substantial transcriptomic data underlying the common signaling pathways induced by various types of GQDs and pointed out the specific toxicity of A-GQDs on hemostatic system.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are newly-emerged graphene-derived nanoparticles (NPs) with lateral dimensions typically below 10 nm in sizes, and show better performance in electronic, optical, spin, and photoelectric properties induced by the quantum confinement effect and edge effect (Yan et al., 2019). GQDs hold great promise in biomedical applications for their high biocompatibilities (Zhao et al., 2020), tunable photoluminescence (PL) (Lu et al., 2020), light-dependent anti- (pro-) oxidant activities (Chong et al., 2016), and intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activities (Lin et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2015), which have been exploited as sensitive fluorophores for real-time bioimaging and anticancer photodynamic therapy in in vivo systems (Ge et al., 2014; Lu et al., 2019; Zhang and Ding, 2018), and also independently used for anti-inflammation via their strong free radical scavenging activities (Lee et al., 2021, 2020; Ren et al., 2018; Tosic et al., 2019). Interestingly, two recent studies found that GQDs could specifically disaggregate amyloid proteins (i.e. α-synuclein fibrils, islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP)) and reduce the neurological toxicity in Parkinson’s disease as well as improve IAAP-related symptoms (Kim et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018).

Although most studies claimed that GQDs are well biocompatible and cause low toxicity in organisms, there remain a few inconsistent findings regarding the potential toxicity of GQDs that may result from nanofabrication methods and the varying surface characters, including nanosizes, surface charges, functional groups, element doping, and impurities (Ou et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016). It is now generally believed that hydroxylated GQDs (OH-GQDs) are hazardous for its inducibility of oxidative stress even at a relatively low exposure concentration (25 µg/mL), leading DNA damage, G0/G1 cell cycle arrest, the disruption of microtubule structure, enhanced intestinal penetrability, and activation of multiple signaling pathways (Li et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2019). At present, nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and aminated GQDs (A-GQDs) have been investigated much for their toxicity, although some previous studies asserted that they were low toxic to cell survival and animal development (Li et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2013; Xie et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2015). Most toxicity data about the two GQDs were evidenced from their effects on cellular micromorphology, nucleotide structures, and in vitro gene expression profiling. Both were found to induce DNA methylation and transcriptional changes in relation to the canonical MAPK signaling pathway (Deng et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2019). In addition, it was reported that N-GQDs could deform the structure of lipid droplets, disrupt the redox-sensitive system, cause ferroptosis, and disturb calcium homeostasis (Deng et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2015b; Wu et al., 2020, 2022a, 2022b), while A-GQDs (200 µg/mL) could induce DNA cleavage via H-bonding and π-π stacking (Xu et al., 2018). In general, the low toxicity of most types of GQDs was concluded from the conventional toxicity testing, involving the assessment of cell growth and survival, the developmental indices in animal models, and the canonical blood/molecular markers (Chong et al., 2014; Li et al., 2019; Nurunnabi et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2019), and there remains fragmentary whether all GQDs share the common toxicity pathways and to what extent various types of GQDs perturb specific responses of gene clusters, proteins, metabolites, and behaviors. Several studies as mentioned above have employed toxicomics approaches to reveal that multiple signaling pathways could be significantly regulated by N-GQDs and/or A-GQDs, including coagulation and complement system, cytochrome P450 system, mTOR, ErbB, etc. (Deng et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2019). However, the relevant toxicity assessment of GQDs using high-throughput screening methods based on in vivo systems remains scarce (Deng et al., 2018).

Zebrafish as an ideal animal model has been extensively used in the biosafety evaluation of a wide range of engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) (Chakraborty et al., 2016). Compared to the mouse model, zebrafish exhibits unique characteristic in its high-fecundity that provides considerable number of embryos for high-throughput drug screening (Chakraborty et al., 2009). Zebrafish has been widely applied for the toxicity assessment of a series of carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes (Cheng et al., 2007), carbon fullerene (mainly C60) (Usenko et al., 2007), graphene (Manjunatha et al., 2018), graphene oxide (Chen et al., 2016), carbon quantum dots (Chousidis et al., 2020), and N-GQDs (Deng et al., 2018, 2019; Zhang et al., 2017). This study revealed the common and specific signaling pathways induced by four types of GQDs and provides valuable insights into the potential side effects of GQDs on in vivo transcriptomic responses, and also useful data referenced for the design of more well-biocompatible GQDs.

Four commercial GQDs were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd (XFNANO, Nanjing, China), respectively as graphene quantum dots (GQDs, XF215), graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs, XF074), carboxylated GQDs (COOH-GQDs, XF090), and aminated GQDs (NH2-GQDs, XF092), all of which have been reported for biosafety assessment and drug therapies in previous studies (Hu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019; Ren et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2021, 2020; Xie et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2018, 2019). Except GOQDs made from top-down method (GQDs are fabricated from particular carbon source materials, e.g. carbon fiber, graphene, graphene oxide), the other three GQDs were all fabricated using bottom-up method (synthesis of GQDs from small organic molecules). TEM images of the four GQDs were obtained by a Transmission Electron Microscope (Talos F200X, FEI, USA) under the operating voltage of 200 kV. XPS data of four GQDs were acquired from Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The surface charges of four GQDs in three mediums (ultrapure water (ddH2O), 1 × PBS (pH 7.4, Gibco), and 1 × DMEM (Gibco)) were measured using the Zetasizer Nano ZS apparatus (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, U.K.).

From the larval stage, a batch of wild-type zebrafish (AB) were raised at 28 ℃ and a constant photoperiod of 14: 10 h (light: dark) according to the standard breeding protocol (Detrich et al., 2011). Zebrafish at approximately 4 months old were coupled for spawning and the newly-laid eggs were collected at four hour post fertilization. Exposure assays were carried out with different GQDs in a concentration gradient (50–100–200 µg/mL). Fifty embryos were slightly pipetted into every well of 6-well cell plates filled with 3 mL of GQDs solutions, which were newly prepared every day. Each treatment was performed for at least three biological replicates, and the control group was only treated with water. Survival rates of zebrafish embryos exposed to different GQDs were recorded at 2 day post fertilization (dpf) until to the end of the exposure assay. Malformations including pericardial edema (PE), bent spine (BS), vitelline cyst (VC), and swimming bladder deficiency (SBD) were recorded at 6 dpf and the malformation rates ( %) were calculated. Heartbeat rates (beats/min) were observed and monitored using an automatic counter under an inverted microscope at 3 dpf and 4 dpf, each treatment was carried out with at least three independent replicates. After exposure for 7 d, zebrafish embryos were collected in 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes from each well and kept at − 80 ℃.

Transgenic zebrafish strain Tg (fli: EGFP) expressing green fluorescent proteins driven by fli promoter in endothelial cells of the vasculature was used to investigate the potential side effects of GQDs on circulation system. Transgenic zebrafish adults were obtained from China Zebrafish Resource Center and maintained under the same rearing condition as described in 2.2. After copulation and oviposition, zebrafish embryos were collected and prepared for GQDs exposure assays. Zebrafish at 4 dpf were visualized using an invert fluorescent microscope (Nikon SMZ18, Japan) after exposure to different GQDs at 100 µg/mL. The trunk vasculatures along the mid-trunk area of zebrafish embryos were analyzed using ImagePro Plus 6.0 software.

Total RNAs were extracted from zebrafish embryos using TRIzol® Reagent according to the manufacturer’s instruction (Invitrogen). Genomic DNA debris was degraded using DNase I RNase-free (Takara, Japan). RNA quality was evaluated on 1 % agarose gels and its concentration was measured by means of Nanodrop-2000 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Finally, RNA integrity was monitored using a 2100 Bioanalyzer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA), and only high-quality RNA samples (OD260/280 = 1.8–2.2, OD260/230 ≥ 2.0, RIN ≥ 7, 28 S:18 S ≥ 1.0, quantity > 5 μg) were applied for constructing sequencing libraries. RNA-seq transcriptome library was prepared following TruSeqTM RNA sample preparation Kit from Illumina (San Diego, CA) using 1 μg of total RNA. Shortly, messenger RNA was isolated according to polyA selection method by oligo (dT) beads and then fragmented by fragmentation buffer firstly. Secondly, double-stranded cDNA was synthesized using a SuperScript double-stranded cDNA synthesis kit (Invitrogen, CA) with random hexamer primers (Illumina). Then the synthesized cDNA was subjected to end-repair, phosphorylation and ‘A′ base addition according to Illumina’s library construction protocol. Libraries were size selected for cDNA target fragments of 300 bp on 2 % Low Range Ultra Agarose followed by PCR amplified using Phusion DNA polymerase (NEB) for 15 PCR cycles. After quantified by TBS380, paired-end RNA-seq sequencing library was sequenced with the Illumina HiSeq xten/NovaSeq 6000 sequencer (2 × 150 bp read length).

Principal component analysis (PCA) was performed for mRNA data with OmicShare tools, a free online platform for data analysis (http://www.omicshare.com/tools). The transcripts per million (TPM) values were employed to quantify mRNA expression levels, respectively. The number of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) was screened out using DESeq2. Gene ontology (GO) and KEGG pathways of DEGs were analyzed with the online DAVID Bioinformatics Resources 6.8 and KOBAS 3.0 software, with a Bonferroni-corrected P-value ≤ 0.05 compared with the whole-transcriptome background. Heatmap of DEGs among different treatment was drawn using ggplot2. Protein-protein interaction (PPI) network was deduced by means of online String tool and Cytoscape (Version 3.8.0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of calcium and ATP levels in zebrafish embryos
ce:section.ce:para: The calcium levels were detected using Fluo-3 AM probe (Beyotime, Beijing, China) according to the previously reported method (Jiang et al., 2019). The zebrafish embryos at 7 dpf were sampled and anesthetized with 0.05 % (w/v) MS-222 solution, and then homogenized with 0.9 % physiological saline (w/v) on ice. The homogenate (1: 10 w/v) was centrifuged at 3000g for 10 min at 4 °C, and then the supernatant was filtered using 0.22 µm microfilter membranes and collected for ATP activity assays. Each treatment was performed with 30 embryos for four biological replicates. The fluorescent intensity was automatically read by Infinite M200 Pro (Tecan, Switzerland), and the excitation and emission wavelength was set at 488 nm and 525 nm, respectively. ATP levels of zebrafish embryos were measured using a chemiluminescence ATP determination Kit (Beyotime, China, Cat: S0027). Protein extraction and concentration assays were performed according to the standard protocol provided by the kit, and the final fluorescent intensity was measured using the luminometer program set in Infinite M200 Pro (Tecan, Switzerland). Sample strategy for ATP detection of zebrafish embryos was the same to that for calcium measurement.

Zebrafish embryos exposed to four GQDs for 7 days were collected in centrifuge tubes (2 mL) and gently washed with clean water for at least three times. To avoid protein degeneration during the extraction process, zebrafish embryos were immersed into the liquid nitrogen. After the gradually diminishing of the liquid nitrogen, the tubes were added with an extraction buffer (10 mM Tris, pH7.4; 2 % Triton-X 100; 1 mM PMSF; 1 mM aprotinin; 1 mM leupeptin; 1 mM trypsin inhibitor) and the embryos were homogenized. The homogenate was shaken and incubated on ice for at least 15 min and centrifuged at 12, 000 × rpm for 10 min at 4 ℃, and the supernatant was pipetted into another new centrifuge tubes. Protein concentrations were quantified using the canonical BCA method and the Western blotting (WB) analysis was performed according to the standard protocol. The first antibody CAMKIIα (AF6493) was purchased from Affinity Biosciences (Beijing, China) and Casp8 (GTX54182) were ordered from Genetex (Irvine, CA, USA). The second antibody β-tublin (E021160) was bought from MBL (Nagoya, Japan).

The first-strand cDNA was transcribed with Fastking RT Kit with gDNase (KR116, TIANGEN, Beijing) for mRNAs according to the manufacturer's instructions. qPCR of mRNA expression levels was performed on a Roche LightCycler 480 II (LC480, Roche Diagnostics) in a 20 μL reaction. Primer pairs used for selected genes were shown in Table S1. The relative mRNA levels were normalized to the internal control ef1α and calculated using the 2-ΔΔCt method. Data were shown as Mean ± SD from four independent biological replicates.

Data analysis was performed uing SPSS 22.0 software (IBM, USA), independent sample t-test or One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to determine the significant differences between control and different GQDs treatment. A p value < 0.05 was considered to be statistically significant.

As shown in Fig. 1A, the TEM images showed that the four kinds of GQDs gave a narrow size distribution with well monodisperse, which indicated their high quality. Clear lattice fringes exhibited their graphitic structure with lattice spacing (d = 0.21 nm, 0.24 nm) corresponding to (100) diffraction facets of graphite. Data of XPS full spectrum scan showed that GOQDs were among the most complex nanoparticles in the compositions of atomic ratios, including C1s (44.21 %), O1s (34.59 %), Na1s (6.79 %), Si2p (8 %), Cl2p (2.44 %), S2p (2.28 %), and Sc2p (6.11 %), whilst C-GQDs contained only C1s (58.13 %) and O1s (41.87 %) (Fig. 1B and Supplemental Table S2). We performed the curve fitting analysis based on the individual elemental scanning, mainly C1s and O1s (Fig. 1C). C1s spectra of R-GQDs and A-GQDs were deconvoluted into five peaks of C-C/CC (~284.6 eV), C-N (~285.7 eV), C-O (~286.6 eV, from C-O or C-O-C), CO (~288.0 eV), and OC-O (~288 eV). The percentage composition of CO was higher than that of C-O in A-GQDs, while R-GQDs showed the opposite compositional change of CO and C-O. Both GOQDs and C-GQDs were characteristic of four components in their C1s spectra, including C-C/CC, C-O, CO, and OC-O. The carboxyl OC-O (~533.6 eV) was also observed in the O1s profile of GOQDs and C-GQDs, along with C = 0 (~531.5 eV) and C-O (~532.7 eV, from C-O or C-O-C), whilst the O1s spectra of R-GQDs and A-GQDs were only composed of CO and C-O. In addition, we made a XPS spectrum scan for the N1s profile of A-GQDs and it was divided into three strong peaks of N-H (~399.2 eV), N-CO (~399.7 eV), and NHn + (400.8 eV) (Supplemental Fig. 1). Next, zeta potential analysis showed that the four GQDs were all negatively charged in three mediums (Fig. 1D), including ddH2O, 1 × PBS, and 1 × DMEM. Among the four NPs, C-GQDs induced the weakest zeta potential changes in the three mediums, ranging from − 1~ − 5 eV.

During the exposure period, the survival rates of zebrafish embryos were not significantly changed by R-GQDs, GOQDs, and C-GQDs when exposed to three concentrations (50, 100, and 200 µg/mL) ( Fig. 2A-C), but notably decreased when treated by A-GQDs at both 100 and 200 µg/mL from 4 dpf (Fig. 2D). In A-GQDs treated groups, many abnormal embryos were found at 6 dpf relative to control and other GQDs treatments, and PE and SBD were identified as the dominant deformed characters in A-GQDs treatment (Supplemental Fig. 2). Heart rates of zebrafish embryos at 3 dpf were not significantly changed by all GQDs treatments (Fig. 2E), but decreased notably in embryos exposed to 100 and 200 µg/mL of A-GQDs at 4 dpf when compared to the control group (Fig. 2F).

We firstly made the principal component analysis (PCA) for mRNA-seq data among different treatment. PC1 scores of A-GQDs were clearly distinct from those of other treatments regardless of mRNA data (p < 0.001). The mRNA expression datasets of control, GQDs, and C-GQDs clustered in close distances on PC2 axis of PCA scores plots, while the PC2 scores of R-GQDs were slightly deviated from the above three treatments, but not to a significant level ( Fig. 3A). With the same corrected p value (FDR) < 0.05 and two cutoff settings (fold changes (FC), FC > 1.5), two ensembles of DEGs were screened out between control and various GQDs treatment. A-GQDs elicited the most number of DEGs (FC > 1.5, 4796), far more than the total of DEGs induced in other three GQDs treatments (Fig. 3B, Supplemental Table S3). To find out the common transcriptomic responses shared by different treatment, the Venn analysis based on DEGs data across four GQDs treatment was performed with a FC cutoff value > 1.5 and a significant FDR value of < 0.05, and 75 DEGs were identified to be shared by the four GQDs treatments (Fig. 3C, Supplemental Table S4).

Conventionally, we performed the functional enrichment analysis of Gene Ontology (GO) for DEGs identified from different GQDs treatment. With threshold value as FDR < 0.05, more than 500 subcategories were found to be significantly enriched in A-GQDs treated group, encompassing biological process (BP, 329 terms), cellular component (CC, 86 terms), and molecular function (MF, 91 terms). In contrast, the significantly enriched GO items were less found in C-GQDs (66), GOQDs (48), and R-GQDs treatment (48) (Supplemental Table S5). In the top ten GO terms from A-GQDs and GOQDs treated groups, both shared two GO terms – ‘cellular response to unfolded protein and response to unfolded protein’ ( Fig. 4A). To better explore GO and associated DEGs commonly induced by different GQDs, we performed a Venn analysis based on the significantly enriched GO terms generalized from different treatment, and two GO terms – ‘ion membrane transport’ and ‘ion transport’ were found to be commonly aroused by the four GQDs (Fig. 4B). Base on the two Go terms, the Venn analysis identified six and ten DEGs shared by the four GQDs in each GO term (Fig. 4C and D), and finally 8 genes were determined from these DEGs, including gria4b, kcnt1, kcnn1a, cacna1ba, kcnma1a, cacna1db, abcc5, and apoa4b.2 (Fig. 3E). Except the up-regulated expression of apoa4b.2, other genes related to potassium (K+) and calcium (Ca2+) channels were significantly suppressed by the four GQDs. To examine whether the transcriptional changes of genes related to ion channels disturb the ion homeostasis, we used the Fluo-3 AM probe to detect the changes of the calcium signals across all treatments, and found that the fluorescent intensity decreased in the four GQDs treatment when compared to that in control group, but not to a significant level (Fig. 4F, p > 0.05). Although the changes of calcium and potassium levels are intimated with ATP energy production, there were no significant changes of the ATP levels between control and different GQDs treatment (Fig. 4G). However, we found that the protein expression of CAMKIIα, a key gene responsible for calcium homeostasis and glycogen metabolism notably attenuated in GOQDs and A-GQDs treatments when compared to that in control (Fig. 4H and I).

Similar to GO enrichment trends among different GQDs treatment, 11 KEGG pathways were identified to be significantly enriched in A-GQDs treated group, more than the total number of pathways enriched in other GQDs treatments ( Fig. 5A, Supplemental Table S6). ‘MAPK’ and ‘Cell adhesion molecules (CAM)’ enriched in A-GQDs treatment involved more than 200 DEGs, and the two pathways have important functions in inflammatory signaling. We made an overall pathway analysis based on 75 DEGs commonly induced by the four GQDs (Fig. 3C), which exhibited a congruous expression profiling within three biological replicates between control and treatment groups (Fig. 5B). To investigate whether these overlapping genes interacted with each other, we carried out GO and KEGG analysis, and identified ‘protein N-acetylglucosaminyltransferase’ and ‘RNA processing’ as the significantly enriched GO terms, and ‘Splicesome’ as the only significantly enriched KEGG pathway (Supplemental Table S7). Accordingly, we performed interactive analysis based on these overlapping genes and showed that serine/arginine-rich splicing factor 11 (srfs11) was the highly interconnected node in the gene interaction network (the inserted table in Fig. 5C), and as the center mediator coordinating with other splicing factors to regulate downstream genes, including nktr, hspbp1, rbm5, gpatch8, and arglu1a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The disruptive effect of A-GQDs on hemostatic system
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above functional analysis, A-GQDs were found to trigger strong transcriptomic responses relative to other three GQDs, and the gene interaction network was established on the basis of DEGs from those significantly enriched KEGG pathway in A-GQDs treatment. As shown in Fig. 6A, the network was mainly composed of two clusters (Cluster 1 and Cluster 2). Cluster1 was a collection of genes associated with complement and coagulation system, and Cluster 2 was a mixed group involving MAPK, CAMs, and apoptotic signals. Noticeably, DEGs from the above pathways were tightly related to the anticoagulant protein C (PC) pathway, which plays multifunctional roles in vascular hemostasis by maintaining vascular barrier integrity and protecting host immune system in response to vascular inflammation. Typical genes in PC pathway including proca, f5, f7, thbd, c3a, c5a, etc. were identified to be highly up-regulated by A-GQDs at 100 μg/mL, while few genes in the pathway were significantly regulated by other GQDs (Fig. 6B). PC pathway usually interacts with hematopoietic transcription factors to maintain vascular hemostasis. For example, the deficiency of endothelia Fli1 would lead the formation of multiple thrombi and a significant reduction in platelet numbers in peripheral blood. Thus, we investigated the Fli expression using Tg (Fli1: EGFP) transgenic zebrafish embryos exposed to different GQDs at 100 μg/mL at 4d (Fig. 6C), and found that the relative fluorescence intensity (RFI) increased in A-GQDs treated embryos at 4 dpf when compared to the control and other GQDs treatments (Fig. 6D), but not to a significant level (p = 0.062).

ce:section.ce:section-title: qRT-PCR assays and WB for A-GQDs toxicity on hemostasis
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the adverse effect of A-GQDs on hemostasis, we firstly performed qRT-PCR to examine the mRNA changes of nine genes from PC pathway and the apoptotic signaling pathway. Results showed that almost all genes were transcriptionally up-regulated by A-GQDs in a dose-dependent manner, and proca and plg were found to be notably induced by A-GQDs even at the low concentration (50 μg/mL) ( Fig. 7A). We also did qPCR analysis for the nine genes in zebrafish embryos exposed to other three GQDs at 200 μg/mL and found that few of these genes were significantly aroused. Next, the gene interaction network deduced from A-GQDs induced DEGs showed that apoptotic pathway was interconnected with PC pathway (Fig. 6A), and then we detected the levels of the apoptotic initiator-CASP8 using zebrafish embryos exposed to the four GQDs at 100 μg/mL and found the higher expression level of CASP8 in A-GQDs treatment (100 μg/mL) than that in control and other GQDs treatment (Fig. 7B-C).

In the study, we extended the number of GQDs for toxicity evaluation, and four types of GQDs including R-GQDs, GOQDs, C-GQDs, and A-GQDs were investigated for their potential toxic effects on in vivo transcriptional changes for the first time. Our exposure assay exhibited that A-GQDs caused the development arrest of zebrafish embryos when exposure concentration was above 100 μg/mL, while no developmental impairment was observed in other three GQDs treatment even at the highest concentration (200 μg/mL). With the same four GQDs, a previous study showed their low toxicity on mice (Li et al., 2019), and the inconsistent data about A-GQDs toxicity may result from the differential sensitivity of two animal models to chemicals (Inoue et al., 2016) or the different exposure assays. However, a recent study showed that A-GQDs could cause fibrotic effects on lung, liver and kidney of mice (Wu et al., 2022a, 2022b).

GQDs show metal-like attributes and have been reported to possess anti- and pro-oxidant activities (Chong et al., 2016). Our functional enrichment analysis revealed that the four GQDs can disturb ion homeostasis by co-suppressing K+ and Ca2+ channels-associated gene clusters (Fig. 4), which was partly proven in the decreased calcium levels and CAMKIIα expression levels induced by different GQDs treatment, then suggesting that GQDs may involve in the ion homeostasis via the energy and charge transfer. The inhibitory effects of GQDs on the transcriptional levels of K+ and Ca2+ channels were also observed in the mouse lung and the primary cortical neurons of rats treated with graphene oxide nanosheets (Bramini et al., 2016; Park et al., 2017). A recent study used the molecular dynamics simulation to find that GQDs can cover the channel entrance of Kir3.2 (a.k.a KCNJ6) and decrease the channel current in a concentration-dependent manner (Gu et al., 2021), then indicating that ion channels may be universally regulated by various graphene-based nanomaterials, but to varying degrees upon their surface characterization. Meanwhile, blocking K+ and Ca2+ channels can jointly or independently delay the action potential repolarization and intensify the blood vessel dilatation, and sometimes trigger irregular physiological responses in the muscle and cardiovascular system (Kitazono et al., 1995; Rudy, 2008). Correspondingly, our study showed that A-GQDs decreased the heart rate of zebrafish embryos to a significant level when compared to control and other GQDs treatment, and exerted strong inhibitory effects on the expression levels of CAMKIIα, which is a calcium-regulatory protein (Bayer and Schulman, 2019), implying that A-GQDs could be a potent ion channel antagonists. However, more engineered methods will be required to improve the biocompatibility of A-GQDs when used for in vivo therapies. Importantly, owing to the universally regulatory effects of different GQDs on K+ and Ca2+ channels, it is very necessary to investigate the behavioral changes of animals when exposed to different GQDs in the future.

Our finding about the transcriptional regulation of spliceosomes triggered by GQDs was also reported in A549 cell line and zebrafish treated with GO (Liao et al., 2020; Zou et al., 2018), implying that the pathway could be commonly aroused by GQDs and other graphene derivatives. Splicing factors such as splicing factors 3b (SF3B) have been reported as a core target for anticancer treatment (Eymin, 2020), and in this study five splicing factors (srsf2a, sf3b1, srf4, srsf5a, and zgc: 163098) from the spliceosome pathway were all significantly downregulated by the four GQDs, indicating the potential of GQDs as spliceosome inhibitors or adjuvants for targeted cancer therapy.

Among the four GQDs, A-GQDs showed more toxic effects on the in vivo system than other GQDs, and the PC pathway aroused by A-GQDs was also identified in N-GQDs treated zebrafish embryos (Deng et al., 2018). Interestingly, we found that A-GQDs and N-GQDs shared the similar N1s profiles in two peaks corresponding with amide bonds and ammonium ions (Zhang et al., 2017). Therefore, A-GQDs should be functionally improved to lessen its potential genotoxic effects when using for in vivo therapies.

Our study showed that all GQDs can induced the transcriptional changes of gene clusters associated with ion channels (K+/Ca2+) and spliceosomes. In contrast with other three GQDs, A-GQDs showed more toxic effects on in vivo systems via strongly activating PC pathway and downstream apoptotic signaling. This finding provided valuable insights about the common and specific transcriptomic responses aroused by different GQDs.

Animal experiments were carried out by stringently complying with the ‘Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals’ (Eighth Edition, 2011. ILARCLS, National Research Council, Washington, D.C.). The animal protocol was approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of Chongqing in China and by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (Approval ID: ZKCQY0510).

Shun Deng: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data collection, Analysis, Funding acquisition. Enming Zhang: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yan Wang: Investigation. Zezhong Yang: Data curation, Software. Xuangen Deng: Resources support. Hai Shen: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Haibo Rong: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Desheng Pei: Resources support.

This work was supported by the research project from Central Government Funds of Guiding Local Scientific and Technological Development for Sichuan Province (2021ZYD0068), Sichuan Medical Association (S20022), and Fundamental Research Fund for the Central Universities (2015CP001) from Ministry of Education, PRC.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.toxlet.2022.05.006.
